title: GitHub Portfolio Tips
description: Transform your GitHub profile into a compelling portfolio that showcases your technical skills, open-source contributions, and project craftsmanship to recruiters and peers.

sections.title: Profile README
sections.description: A well-crafted Profile README is your GitHub landing page. Include a clear header (name, role, location), tech stack badges (via shields.io), pinned repositories showcasing your best work, contribution statistics (gh-stats), and a call to action (LinkedIn, personal site, email). Keep it concise — recruiters scan in seconds.
sections.codeBlock:
<!-- Profile README key sections -->
# Hello, I'm [Your Name] 👋

**Backend Engineer | Python & Go | Distributed Systems**

[![LinkedIn](https://img.shields.io/badge/-LinkedIn-blue)]()
[![Twitter](https://img.shields.io/badge/-Twitter-1DA1F2)]()

---

### 🔭 Currently working on
- Open-source monitoring tool for Kubernetes

### 📊 GitHub Stats
![GitHub stats](https://github-readme-stats.vercel.app/api?username=yourname&show_icons=true&theme=dark)

sections.title: Repository Curation
sections.description: Quality over quantity. Pin 6 repos that demonstrate range: one full-stack project, one library/tool, one contribution to a popular OSS project, one data/ML project, one DevOps/infra project, and one creative/experimental. Each repo needs: clear README with badges, demo screenshots/GIFs, installation guide, test suite, and CI passing badge.
sections.codeBlock:
# Repository checklist for portfolio quality
- [ ] Descriptive README with problem statement
- [ ] Tech stack badges (build, test, coverage, license)
- [ ] Animated GIF demo or screenshot
- [ ] Quick start: install → configure → run
- [ ] Contributing guide + code of conduct
- [ ] GitHub Actions CI passing
- [ ] Semantic versioning tags and releases
- [ ] Tests (unit + integration) with coverage badge
- [ ] Linting and formatting configured
- [ ] Security policy + dependabot enabled

sections.title: Contributions Showcase
sections.description: Active open-source contributions signal collaboration skills. Focus on quality PRs to well-known projects: small bug fixes, documentation improvements, test additions. Use the GitHub profile's contribution graph to demonstrate consistency. Enable the private contributions display to show all activity. Highlight merged PRs in your profile README.
sections.codeBlock:
# Tracking and showcasing contributions

# Find beginner-friendly issues
git log --all --oneline --grep="good first issue"

# Show merged PRs (via GitHub CLI)
gh pr list --state merged --limit 10 --author @me

# Profile README contribution section
### 📦 Open Source Contributions
- [react](https://github.com/facebook/react/pull/1234) Fixed edge case in hooks
- [kubernetes](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/5678) Updated monitoring docs
- [pandas](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/9012) Added test coverage for groupby

sections.title: Project Documentation
sections.description: Great documentation separates a hobby project from a portfolio piece. Structure: README.md (what, why, how), CONTRIBUTING.md, CHANGELOG.md (keep a changelog), LICENSE, SECURITY.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md. Use GitHub wiki for extended docs, GitHub Pages for hosted documentation, and GitHub Discussions for community Q&A.
sections.codeBlock:
# Standard project documentation files
README.md          # Project overview, badges, install, usage
CONTRIBUTING.md    # How to contribute, code style, PR process
CHANGELOG.md       # Keep a Changelog format (https://keepachangelog.com)
LICENSE            # MIT, Apache-2.0, or GPL-3.0
SECURITY.md        # How to report vulnerabilities
CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md # Contributor Covenant

# Example CHANGELOG.md entry
## [1.2.0] - 2024-03-15
### Added
- OAuth2 authentication support (#45)
### Fixed
- Memory leak in WebSocket handler (#42)

sections.title: Networking
sections.description: Engage with the GitHub community: follow developers in your field, star repos you learn from, comment on issues with solutions (not just +1), and submit thoughtful PR reviews. Join GitHub Sponsors to support maintainers. Use GitHub Discussions in relevant communities. Attend virtual meetups and share what you learn via gists or blog posts linked from your profile.
sections.codeBlock:
# Effective GitHub networking checklist
- [ ] Follow 10+ engineers in your domain
- [ ] Star and watch repos for learning
- [ ] Answer 1 question/week on GitHub Discussions
- [ ] Review 1 open PR/week with constructive feedback
- [ ] Write 1 technical gist/month
- [ ] Share your GitHub profile on LinkedIn, resume, portfolio
- [ ] Join GitHub Sponsors as a supporter
- [ ] Enable GitHub Achievements on profile

details: Recruiters spend an average of 7 seconds on a GitHub profile before deciding to engage. Key signals they scan for: contribution recency (activity within last 30 days), code quality in pinned repos, README clarity, test presence, and community engagement. A profile with consistent contributions, well-documented repos, and visible collaboration outperforms one with many repos but shallow content. Aim for the 'green wall' — at least one contribution per day maintains the streak and signals discipline.
cheatSheet:
Core Practices

  • Profile README — name, role, tech stack badges, pinned repos, GitHub stats card.
  • 6 pinned repos — range: full-stack, library, OSS contribution, data/ML, DevOps, experimental.
  • Repo quality — README, badges, tests, CI passing, demo GIF, CHANGELOG, LICENSE.
  • Green wall — at least one contribution per day maintains the streak.

Staff+ Patterns

  • Quality over quantity — a well-documented repo with tests outperforms 10 shallow repos.
  • Contribute to well-known OSS projects — 3 good PRs to React/Kubernetes/TensorFlow signal more than 20 personal projects.
  • Enable private contribution display on profile to show all activity.

Watch Outs

  • Empty pinned repos or "Hello World" projects damage credibility. Only pin complete, maintained work.
  • Recruiters spend ~7 seconds scanning a profile. If your README is empty, they move on.
  • Fake contribution streaks (automated commits) are easily detected and backfire.

Interview Angle

Recruiters across FAANG use GitHub profiles as a pre-screen signal. Key signals: recency (last 30 days), code quality in pinned repos, README clarity, test presence, and community engagement. A profile with consistent contributions and visible collaboration outperforms one with many repos but shallow content.
